story_summary: After leaving his executive role in 1997, the narrator built a third career as a corporate board member, consultant and adjunct professor, through a sequence of board appointments, entrepreneurial investments, consulting engagements and teaching. He experienced notable successes (Alliance Data, Fed board, consulting retainers) and failures (Laundromax), and learned governance, risk and professional lessons.

context.role: Independent director, strategic consultant, adjunct professor
context.industry: Financial services, corporate governance, consulting, education
context.stakes: Personal reputation, fiduciary responsibilities, invested capital (e.g., $400,000 loss), board-level governance and organizational outcomes

core_conflict: Balancing opportunities to serve on boards and advise companies (with attendant reputational, time and financial commitments) against the risks of entrepreneurial failure, market forces outside one’s control, and ethical governance decisions that could require opposition or resignation.

decision_moments.trigger: Invitation from the Federal Reserve Bank to join the Miami branch board.
constraints: I never learned exactly how I was selected for this post.
Board service carries time and fiduciary commitment; term limits applied.
options: Accept the invitation and serve on the board.
decision_moments.choice: Accepted; served two three-year terms (six years) until term-limited out.
decision_moments.reasoning: Serving on its Miami board was one of the more enjoyable experiences... I got a practical education about economics and monetary policy and felt that I made a genuine contribution.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Time commitment and public profile associated with Fed board membership.
decision_moments.outcome: Valuable experience, networking, enhanced reputation in South Florida business community.

decision_moments.trigger: Recommendation from Tom Saporito to join the board of RHR International, a firm of corporate psychologists.
constraints: All of the partner/shareholders in this firm were psychologists and not well trained in business matters.
options: Join as an independent director and contribute business/consulting discipline.
decision_moments.choice: Joined and served about ten years, contributing to operations.
decision_moments.reasoning: I had worked with consultants at KPMG for more than thirty years and knew that discipline through and through. For this reason, I think I made a significant contribution...
decision_moments.outcome: Positive, enjoyable experience; contributed business discipline to a psychology-run firm.

decision_moments.trigger: Approach in 1999 by young entrepreneurs to join a new mutual fund (CRA Qualified Investment Fund).
constraints: Founders were in their early thirties with youth and inexperience.
Highly regulated mutual fund focused on federally mandated funding for low-income properties.
options: Join as independent director despite doubts.
Decline due to concerns about founders' experience and regulatory complexity.
decision_moments.choice: Joined as an independent director; served for several years before leaving.
decision_moments.reasoning: I met with them and was impressed with their enthusiasm and unbridled commitment... I was intrigued with the concept for some reason and agreed to do it.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Board service amid regulatory complexity and founders' inexperience.
decision_moments.outcome: Venture was quite successful; the fund grew to about $2 billion in assets and a pristine reputation.

decision_moments.trigger: Approach by entrepreneurs Alan Haig and Eric Lambert to invest in and join board of Laundromax (build 600 laundromats).
constraints: Ambitious capital needs to reach breakeven.
Founders were young though academically/professionally capable.
Highly leveraged plan requiring continued access to capital markets.
options: Invest and join board (support expansion plan).
Decline to invest given capital-market risks and founders' youth.
decision_moments.choice: Invested ($400,000) and served on board; later became chairman.
decision_moments.reasoning: I processed these guys through my tried and true formula for judging people —intelligence, energy and integrity —and they passed the test.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Personal capital investment and reputational exposure on a high-growth plan.
decision_moments.outcome: Business failed when capital markets dried up in 2000; investors lost entire investment; learned that some things are uncontrollable.

decision_moments.trigger: Weariness with a few board members perceived as small-minded at CRA Qualified Investment Fund.
constraints: Interpersonal friction with some board members.
Opportunity cost of staying versus pursuing more interesting roles.
options: Remain on the board and manage friction.
decision_moments.choice: Resigned to move on to something more interesting.
decision_moments.reasoning: I became weary of dealing with a few board members who, I felt, were being small-minded, and resigned to move on to something more interesting for me.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Removing oneself from an ongoing successful venture and associated influence.
decision_moments.outcome: Left the board and pursued other board and consulting opportunities.

decision_moments.trigger: Invitation in 2002 from Alan Levan to join BankAtlantic Bancorp board; subsequent proposal of a $30 million executive compensation package in early 2013.
constraints: Chairman Alan had a controlling nature; other directors generally agreeable.
Economic and regulatory pressures weakening the bank over years.
options: Oppose the package and remain on board.
decision_moments.choice: Voted against the compensation and then resigned as a director of both companies.
decision_moments.reasoning: I had a serious disagreement... over what I thought was an outrageous $30 million executive compensation package... I voted against the compensation and then resigned.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Leaving influential board positions and potential loss of platform; but preserved principles.
decision_moments.outcome: Resigned professionally and without fanfare; remained on friendly terms and received an 18-month retainer for advice.

decision_moments.trigger: Request from Roger Ballou (CDI) to find a buyer for Modern Engineering subsidiary, with a success fee if closed within six months.
constraints: Six-month deadline for signing a buyer to earn the success fee.
options: Accept the assignment and search for buyers.
decision_moments.choice: Accepted, traveled to Detroit, and sold the company within six months.
decision_moments.reasoning: We worked out an agreement whereby I would be paid a per diem fee... with a success fee of $100,000 if I could sign a buyer within six months.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Time and travel; contingent fee dependent on timely sale.
decision_moments.outcome: Sale closed within six months; collected success fee.

decision_moments.trigger: Invitation to teach as an adjunct professor at Nova Southeastern University (circa 2003); initial reluctance followed by encouragement from a friend and spouse.
constraints: Initial lack of interest to teach; time commitment.
options: Try teaching one course as an experiment.
decision_moments.choice: Taught one course and then continued teaching Entrepreneurial Finance twice a year for about ten years.
decision_moments.reasoning: T om leaned on me hard... I consulted with Dot, who encouraged me. So I took the plunge and agreed to teach one course.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Time commitment and learning curve in academic delivery.
decision_moments.outcome: Taught over 500 students, enjoyed it, received excellent ratings, and continued as guest lecturer after retirement from regular teaching.

principles.principle: Judge founders and leaders by intelligence, energy and integrity.
Accept that external market forces can make outcomes unmanageable despite sound planning.
principles.evidence: Some things in life are just not controllable, no matter how much effort is expended.

principles.principle: Maintain professional conduct when disagreeing or exiting roles.
principles.evidence: I handled this disagreement and resignation professionally and without fanfare,

principles.principle: Use failures as teaching tools and case studies to pass lessons to others.
principles.evidence: Nova Southeastern reasoned that their students needed to be schooled in failures as well.

principles.principle: Leverage personal and professional networks to create opportunities and solutions.
principles.evidence: I accidentally stumbled upon a guy who expressed a keen interest in acquiring the company.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Screen potential founders/partners for intelligence, energy and integrity before committing.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I processed these guys through my tried and true formula for judging people —intelligence, energy and integrity —and they passed the test.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When market liquidity disappears, recognize limits of control and stop assuming expansion will continue.
decision_heuristics.evidence: The capital markets for entrepreneurial businesses completely dried up, quite suddenly, in the year 2000.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: If a compensation or governance decision conflicts with personal principles, vote your stance and be prepared to resign.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I voted against the compensation and then resigned as a director of both companies.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Accept short-term, fee-based consulting assignments with a clear success metric and act quickly to exploit serendipitous leads.
decision_heuristics.evidence: We worked out an agreement whereby I would be paid a per diem fee for my services with a success fee of $100,000 if I could sign a buyer within six months.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Serve as an independent sounding board for clients who want objective testing of judgment and be willing to maintain long-term retainer relationships.
decision_heuristics.evidence: They simply desire an independent sounding board and a resource to test their judgment against.

leadership_patterns.risk_tolerance: Willing to take measured entrepreneurial and reputational risks (personal investment in startups, varied board roles) while accepting possible losses.
leadership_patterns.communication_style: Professional, candid and collegial; communicates independence and principled disagreement without fanfare.
leadership_patterns.accountability_posture: Accepts fiduciary duty and personal accountability—votes against actions he finds objectionable and resigns when necessary; accepts personal financial loss.
leadership_patterns.ethical_framing: Prioritizes integrity and sound governance; evaluates actions against ethical and professional standards rather than convenience.

quotes.quote: Beats me, I just got a call from Washington and was told that you’re the man!
Some things in life are just not controllable, no matter how much effort is expended.
I handled this disagreement and resignation professionally and without fanfare,
I was hooked —just as T om had predicted.
They simply desire an independent sounding board and a resource to test their judgment against.
